headline: As Deadline Lapses, Trump Administration Continues TikTok Talks

“The Treasury Department is engaging with ByteDance to complete the divestment and other steps necessary to resolve the national security risks,” a Treasury spokesperson said in a statement late Friday.

The lack of immediate enforcement provides TikTok with a significant tactical victory in its effort to run out the clock on an administration that has made the app a centerpiece of its confrontational trade and technology policy toward China. With the inauguration of President-elect Joseph R. Biden Jr. just 46 days away, the urgency that once characterized Mr. Trump’s demand for a sale appears to have dissipated, replaced by a complex legal and regulatory limbo.

One order, based on the International Emergency Economic Powers Act, sought to effectively ban the app from U.S. app stores. The second, issued through CFIUS, ordered ByteDance to sell TikTok’s American assets.

Under this arrangement, Oracle would become TikTok’s “trusted technology provider,” responsible for hosting U.S. user data on its servers.

ByteDance, meanwhile, has sought to maintain a majority stake, and the Chinese government has complicated the proceedings by introducing new export restrictions on the artificial intelligence algorithms that power TikTok’s signature "For You" recommendation feed.

Without those algorithms, which are considered the app’s "secret sauce," analysts say TikTok would be significantly less valuable to a potential buyer.

These legal setbacks have significantly diminished the administration’s leverage. While the CFIUS order to divest remains legally distinct from the Commerce Department’s attempted ban, the government’s inability to shut down the app has made it difficult to force ByteDance into a fire sale.

Within the administration, the approach to TikTok has often been a source of internal friction. The company has pointed to its efforts to hire American executives and its plans to establish a "Transparency Center" in Los Angeles as evidence of its commitment to U.S. security.

In a statement earlier this week, a TikTok spokesperson said the company remained committed to working with the government. “We remain focused on continuing to engage with the government to reach a resolution that addresses their security concerns, even as we disagree with them,” the spokesperson said.

The shift in focus toward the presidential transition has also altered the calculus for ByteDance. While Mr. Biden has expressed concerns about TikTok’s data privacy practices, his transition team has not yet signaled whether it will pursue the same aggressive divestiture strategy as the Trump administration. The Treasury Department has the authority to refer the matter to the Justice Department for enforcement, which could theoretically lead to the seizure of assets. But such a move would be unprecedented for a consumer technology company and would almost certainly trigger a fresh round of litigation.

As the sun set on the Friday deadline, the lack of a formal announcement from the White House or the Treasury Department suggested that, for now, the government is choosing the path of least resistance. The "ticking clock" that Mr. Trump once used to pressure the Chinese tech giant has, at least for the moment, been silenced.

For ByteDance, the strategy of waiting out the administration appears to be yielding results. What was once framed as an urgent national security crisis has evolved into a protracted bureaucratic negotiation, one that may not be resolved until well after the current president has left the Oval Office.

headline: TikTok Pushes Back Against Claims It Fuels Antisemitism

Drivers must navigate a labyrinth of digital health codes and physical temperature checks, often facing a week of quarantine after crossing provincial lines. This friction has sent shipping costs soaring and left warehouses overflowing with goods that have nowhere to go.

Beijing has attempted to grease the wheels of commerce with a flurry of stimulus measures, including tax breaks, cheap loans for small businesses, and directives to local officials to prioritize production. On Tuesday, President Xi Jinping made his first visit to Wuhan, the epicenter of the outbreak, a carefully choreographed trip intended to signal that the country was open for business.

However, there are growing signs that the push for normalcy is creating its own set of distortions. In some regions, local officials, eager to meet Beijing’s quotas for electricity consumption—a proxy for industrial activity—have reportedly ordered factories to keep lights and machinery running even if no workers are present. Such "paper recoveries" suggest that the official statistics may be overstating the pace of the rebound.

"There is a tension between the political necessity of a restart and the epidemiological reality on the ground," said a senior economist at a major Western bank. "Local cadres are being told to eliminate the virus and grow the economy simultaneously. In practice, those two goals are often in direct opposition."

Even if China manages to solve its internal production woes, it now faces a new, external threat: the evaporation of the global consumer. In January and February, the primary concern for the world was that China could not supply goods. By mid-March, the concern has flipped. As the United States and Europe implement social distancing measures, cancel major events, and close their borders, the demand for Chinese-made electronics, apparel, and automobiles is cratering.

The cancelation of orders from Western retailers is already being felt in the Pearl River Delta. Export-oriented firms that survived the initial weeks of the lockdown are now seeing their order books vanish as their customers in Milan, New York, and Seattle close their doors. 

The automotive sector, a pillar of the Chinese economy, serves as a grim bellwether. Sales of passenger cars in China plunged 80 percent in February, according to the China Passenger Car Association. While some showrooms have reopened, foot traffic remains negligible. Potential buyers, worried about their job security and the possibility of a prolonged recession, are in no mood for big-ticket purchases.

The psychological toll of the virus also remains a formidable barrier to the service sector. In Beijing and Shanghai, restaurants and malls have reopened their doors, but they remain largely empty. Waiters in masks and gloves outnumber diners, and security guards at every entrance brandish infrared thermometers like talismans. The "fear factor" has created a self-imposed quarantine that no government decree can easily lift.

"The government can order the factories to open, but it cannot order people to go to the movies or buy a new car," said a retail analyst in Hong Kong. "Until there is a sense of total safety, the consumer economy will remain in a defensive crouch."

The economic data released so far has been harrowing. China’s official manufacturing purchasing managers’ index (P.M.I.) fell to a record low of 35.7 in February, a figure worse than any recorded during the 2008 financial crisis. Analysts at Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley have slashed their growth forecasts for the first quarter, with some now predicting the first contraction in the Chinese economy since the end of the Mao era in 1976.

For the rest of the world, the difficulty of China’s restart is a sobering preview of the months ahead. If a centralized, authoritarian government with the power to mobilize vast resources and enforce strict discipline is struggling to bring its economy back online, the challenge for Western democracies may be even more acute.

As the sun set over the industrial parks of Suzhou this week, the chimneys were smoking again, and the evening air was punctuated by the hum of generators. On the surface, it looked like a country returning to work. But beneath that veneer of activity, the Chinese economy remains a fragile ecosystem, trying to find its footing on ground that is still shifting. 

The Great Shutdown was an act of political will, a desperate measure to contain a biological threat. The Great Restart, by contrast, is a grinding slog against the laws of economics and the persistence of fear. For the global economy, the news from China is clear: stopping the world was the easy part. Starting it again will be the work of years, not weeks.

headline: Halting China’s Economy Was Hard. Restarting It Is Harder.
generated_full_text:
For weeks, the world watched as China performed a feat of authoritarian engineering never before seen in the modern era: it simply turned itself off. To contain the coronavirus, the government shuttered thousands of factories, cordoned off tens of millions of people and effectively severed the arteries of global trade.

Now comes the even more difficult part. 

As the ruling Communist Party declares a tentative victory over the domestic outbreak, it is pivoting to a secondary, perhaps more daunting mission: turning the world’s second-largest economy back on. But the gears of the Chinese industrial machine are not clicking back into place with the same speed at which they were halted. 

Interviews with factory owners, logistics experts and economists, along with a review of satellite data and corporate filings, suggest that China’s recovery is being hampered by a thicket of logistical bottlenecks, labor shortages and a deepening crisis of global demand. “The shutdown was a political command, and in China, political commands are executed with brutal efficiency,” said Arthur Kroeber, head of research at Gavekal Dragonomics. “But a restart is a commercial process. You cannot simply command people to buy cars or command a global supply chain to resume its natural flow when the rest of the world is now going into its own lockdown.”

The challenges begin at the factory gate. While Beijing claims that more than 90 percent of large state-owned enterprises have resumed operations, the reality for the small and medium-sized businesses that form the backbone of the economy is far bleaker. According to surveys by industry groups, many of these firms are operating at less than half capacity.

The primary obstacle is the human element. Hundreds of millions of migrant workers who traveled to their home provinces for the Lunar New Year in January were caught behind a shifting patchwork of provincial checkpoints and quarantine mandates. While the government has chartered special trains and buses to ferry workers back to manufacturing hubs like Guangdong and Zhejiang, the process is agonizingly slow.

Once workers arrive, they are often met with a digital bureaucracy of "health codes"—smartphone apps that dictate their freedom of movement based on their travel history and health status. A "yellow" or "red" code can mean another 14 days of isolation, keeping essential technicians off the assembly lines even as they sit just miles away from their posts.

“We have the orders, and we have the raw materials, but we are missing the people who know how to run the high-precision lathes,” said Wang He, a manager at a specialized electronics firm in Suzhou. “If five people in a 50-person department are missing, the whole line stops.”

Even when the workers are present, the parts often are not. The modern global supply chain is a miracle of "just-in-time" manufacturing, designed to minimize inventory and maximize speed. But that efficiency has become a liability. A single smartphone requires hundreds of components sourced from dozens of suppliers across China and Asia. If a single screw manufacturer in a still-quarantined district of Hubei province remains closed, the final assembly line in Shenzhen remains idle.

Drivers are often reluctant to cross provincial lines for fear of being trapped in a new quarantine or being unable to return home. According to data from G7, a provider of fleet management tools in China, truck activity reached only about 70 percent of 2019 levels by early March.

The restart is also being clouded by a growing skepticism regarding the data being used to measure it. In several industrial hubs, local officials have reportedly pressured factory owners to keep their lights on and machinery running—even if they are not producing anything—to inflate electricity consumption figures. These figures are a key metric used by Beijing to track the recovery, and in the high-stakes world of Chinese provincial politics, meeting a target is often more important than the economic reality it is supposed to represent.

“There is a significant gap between the ‘return to work’ rates and the ‘return to production’ rates,” said Leland Miller, the chief executive of China Beige Book, an independent data provider. “You can have the lights on and the staff in the building, but if you aren't actually making and selling goods, the economy isn't recovering.”

Perhaps the most ominous shadow over the recovery is no longer coming from within China, but from abroad. In January, the crisis was a Chinese supply shock. By March, it has transformed into a global demand shock.

As the United States and Europe begin to implement their own restrictive measures to slow the spread of the virus, the appetite for Chinese-made goods is evaporating. On Wednesday, the World Health Organization officially declared the outbreak a pandemic. Major sporting events have been canceled, travel has been restricted, and consumer confidence in the West is cratering.

Economists at Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley have slashed their growth forecasts for China, with some suggesting that the first quarter could see the country’s first economic contraction in decades. The fear now is that just as China’s factories find their rhythm, they will find they have no one left to sell to.

“China is the first in and the first out of this crisis,” said Alicia García-Herrero, chief economist for Asia-Pacific at Natixis. “But being the first out doesn't help if your customers are now the ones in the hospital. The export sector, which was supposed to lead the recovery, is now facing a wall of cancellations from Europe and America.”

The Chinese government has attempted to cushion the blow through a series of targeted measures, including tax breaks, cheap loans for small businesses, and infrastructure spending. But unlike the massive stimulus package China unleashed during the 2008 global financial crisis, Beijing's current response has been relatively restrained. Officials are wary of further inflating the country’s already massive mountain of corporate and local government debt.

Instead, the government is leaning heavily on a narrative of normalcy. State television broadcasts daily footage of bustling markets and humming factories, emphasizing the message that the "people’s war" against the virus is being won. President Xi Jinping’s visit to Wuhan this week was the ultimate symbolic gesture, intended to signal to both domestic and international audiences that China is open for business.

But for the owners of the small workshops in the coastal export hubs, the reality remains a daily struggle for solvency. Many are facing a "liquidity crunch"—a polite term for running out of cash. Rent and wages must be paid, even if the assembly lines are moving at a crawl and the ships in the harbor are leaving half-empty.

The difficulty of the restart serves as a warning for the rest of the world. If China, with its centralized power and ability to mobilize vast resources, is struggling to revive its economy after a six-week hiatus, the path for Western democracies may be even more treacherous. 

For now, the global economy remains in a state of suspended animation. The "just-in-time" world has discovered that while it is easy to break a chain, rejoining the links requires a level of coordination that the virus has made nearly impossible. 

“We are learning that the global economy is not a car that you can park in the garage and then drive away a month later,” said Mr. Kroeber. “It is an organism. And when you put an organism into a coma, there is no guarantee it wakes up the same as it was before.”

headline: Halting China’s Economy Was Hard. Restarting It Is Harder.

headline: China pushes for regular mass testing in its ‘zero Covid’ pursuit.

For the residents of China’s largest cities, the price of participation in modern society now includes a negative test result every 48 to 72 hours. Without a green QR code on a smartphone, updated with a fresh laboratory result, access to malls, parks, buses, and even one’s own workplace is increasingly denied.

This pivot to "normalized" testing represents one of the most ambitious and expensive public health undertakings in history. But as the costs mount and the economic toll of the pandemic deepens, economists and policy analysts are questioning whether this new phase of the zero-Covid pursuit is a sustainable solution or merely a costly treadmill that will further drain the country’s coffers.

The logistics of the plan are staggering. In Beijing, a city of 22 million, officials have already established more than 9,000 testing sites. In Shanghai, which is only now beginning to emerge from a bruising two-month lockdown that shuttered factories and sparked rare public outcries, authorities have installed nearly 15,000 stations. Smaller cities like Hangzhou and Shenzhen have implemented similar mandates, requiring residents to test every few days regardless of whether an outbreak is active.

The shift is born of necessity. The highly transmissible Omicron variant has rendered China’s previous playbook—targeted lockdowns and contact tracing—largely ineffective. By the time a cluster is identified, the virus has often already moved through several layers of the community. Continuous testing is intended to serve as an early-warning system, catching infections in their incubation phase.

“The government is trying to find a middle path,” said Michael Cheng, a consumer analyst focusing on the Chinese market. “They cannot afford the political cost of moving away from zero-Covid, but they also cannot afford another Shanghai-style lockdown in a city like Beijing. This mass testing is an attempt to keep the economy moving while keeping the virus at bay.”

However, the economic price tag of this "middle path" is becoming a primary concern for the country’s leadership. According to a recent report by analysts at Goldman Sachs, a regular testing program covering all of China’s large and medium-sized cities could cost up to 1 percent of the country’s gross domestic product. Other estimates from Nomura suggest the cost could be even higher, potentially reaching 2 percent of G.D.P. if testing is required every 48 hours nationwide.

The question of who pays for this infrastructure remains fraught. While the central government mandates the policy, the financial burden largely falls on local governments. This comes at a time when municipal budgets are already severely strained by a cooling property market, which has slashed income from land sales—a primary source of revenue for local authorities.

“Local governments are in a vice,” said Victor Shih, a professor at the University of California, San Diego, who studies China’s elite politics and finance. “They are being told to maintain zero-Covid at all costs while also being told to support economic growth. The cost of testing hundreds of millions of people several times a week is a massive fiscal drain that competes with infrastructure spending and social services.”

Despite the strain on public budgets, the testing push has created a gold rush for China’s medical diagnostics industry. Companies that manufacture testing kits and provide laboratory services have reported record-breaking profits. Shares in firms like Andon Health and WuXi Biologics have fluctuated wildly as investors bet on the permanence of the testing regime. In the first quarter of 2022 alone, several major Chinese testing companies reported year-on-year profit increases of over 100 percent.

The boom has led to the emergence of a "testing economy," with some analysts estimating that the market for PCR testing in China could reach $15 billion or more this year. This industry now employs hundreds of thousands of people, from the "dabai"—the "big whites" in protective suits—to the logistics drivers who ferry samples to laboratories around the clock.

Yet, even as the testing booths become a fixture of the urban landscape, the broader economic outlook for China remains grim. On Wednesday, Premier Li Keqiang held an unusual emergency meeting with thousands of local officials, warning that the challenges facing the Chinese economy are in some ways greater than those seen at the start of the pandemic in 2020. He urged officials to strike a better balance between virus control and economic activity, a signal of the growing anxiety within the State Council over the country’s 5.5 percent growth target, which many international analysts now believe is out of reach.

The disruption to supply chains continues to reverberate globally. While factories in Shanghai and the surrounding Yangtze River Delta have begun to restart production under "closed-loop" systems—where workers live on-site to avoid contact with the outside world—logistics remain a bottleneck. Trucking remains difficult as drivers face a patchwork of testing requirements and quarantine rules as they cross provincial lines.

For multinational corporations, the unpredictability of China’s policy is forcing a re-evaluation of their reliance on the country. In recent earnings calls, executives from Apple to Tesla have cited China’s Covid restrictions as a major headwind for their global operations.

Beyond the balance sheets, the social cost of regular mass testing is manifesting as a profound sense of fatigue among the Chinese populace. In Beijing, long lines at testing stations have become a daily grievance, with residents often waiting in the heat for an hour or more to ensure their health codes remain valid.

“It has become a part of your morning routine, like getting coffee,” said Wang Wei, a 34-year-old tech worker in Beijing’s Haidian district. “But there is a constant underlying anxiety. If your result is late, or if there is a mistake in the system, you are essentially locked out of your life. You can’t go to work, you can’t buy groceries. Your freedom depends on a swab.”

The digital surveillance apparatus that supports the testing regime—the smartphone-based health codes—has also raised concerns among human rights advocates. The system, which tracks a user’s location and testing history, represents an unprecedented level of real-time monitoring. There are fears that the infrastructure being built for public health could be repurposed for more permanent forms of social control.

For President Xi Jinping, the success of the mass testing strategy is a matter of immense political importance. With the 20th Party Congress approaching this fall, where Mr. Xi is expected to secure a precedent-breaking third term, the government is determined to avoid the high death tolls seen in the United States and Europe. The state-run media continues to frame the "zero-Covid" policy as a testament to the superiority of China’s governance model.

But as the kiosks become a permanent feature of the Chinese streetscape, the definition of victory is shifting. Victory is no longer the total eradication of the virus, but rather the ability to live in a state of perpetual, high-tech vigilance.

“The 15-minute testing circle is the new normal,” said a public health consultant in Shanghai, who asked not to be named due to the sensitivity of the topic. “The government is betting that the public will accept the inconvenience of a test every two days if it means avoiding a total lockdown. But this is an expensive bet, and they are playing against a virus that doesn’t follow their rules.”

As of late May, the results of this grand experiment remain inconclusive. While case numbers in Shanghai and Beijing have trended downward, new clusters continue to pop up in smaller cities, triggering the same cycle of mass testing and localized restrictions. For the Chinese economy, and the global markets that depend on it, the "zero-Covid" pursuit has entered a new, more clinical, and perhaps more costly phase.

Stand with Hong Kong," a grassroots organization that placed advertisements in international newspapers last year.

For the Chinese government, the legend of Mulan is a story of national unity and the sacrifice of the individual for the greater good of the motherland. State media outlets have frequently used the character to promote patriotic education.

The image of Ms. Chow, standing in the dark outside a police station after her release, has for many become the definitive image of the week — eclipsing the multimillion-dollar marketing campaign for Disney’s film, which is scheduled for release on streaming platforms next month.

For now, Ms. Chow remains in a legal limbo. Though she is out on bail, her passport has been confiscated, and she is required to report to the police regularly. The threat of a formal trial and a lengthy prison sentence looms.

The maintenance issue is particularly acute because these stadiums are often designed to Chinese specifications using Chinese materials. When a specialized scoreboard or a specific hydraulic system breaks, local contractors often lack the parts or the expertise to fix them, requiring expensive service contracts with the original Chinese builders.

Furthermore, the "gift" of a stadium is rarely a simple act of charity. Critics point to the "resource-for-infrastructure" model, where Chinese firms build roads or stadiums in exchange for mining rights. "China is not a donor in the traditional Western sense," said Bradley Parks, the executive director of AidData, a research lab at William & Mary that tracks Chinese development finance. "They are a banker. And like any banker, they expect to be repaid, either in cash or in commodities."

There is also the issue of labor. While Chinese firms have increasingly hired local workers for low-level construction roles, the high-level engineering, architectural, and management positions remain almost exclusively in the hands of Chinese nationals. This limits the "technology transfer" that African governments often cite as a primary benefit of these partnerships.

In Abidjan, however, the geopolitical anxieties feel a world away from the fan zones. For the thousands of Ivorians draped in orange, white, and green, the stadiums are a source of immense national pride. After a decade of political instability and civil unrest, the ability to host a flawless international tournament is seen as a sign that the country has finally turned a corner.

"Look at this place," said Marcelle Koffi, a teacher who traveled from the interior to watch the semi-finals. "People say we shouldn't have spent the money, but for the first time, the whole world is looking at Côte d’Ivoire and seeing something beautiful, not something broken. You cannot put a price on that."

This sentiment is exactly what Beijing counts on. By tapping into the deep cultural passion for soccer, China bypasses the messy, often unpopular conditions attached to Western aid—such as demands for human rights reforms or transparency in governance. 

"The Chinese give us what we want, when we want it, without a lecture," a senior official in the Ivorian Ministry of Sports said in an interview last month. "The West tells us we need better sewers. We know we need sewers. But we also need dignity, and we need a place to play."

As the sun sets over the Ebimpé district, casting long shadows across the Alassane Ouattara Stadium’s concrete buttresses, the lights flicker on—powered by a grid that many Ivorians still find unreliable in their own homes. 

For now, the stadium is a theater of joy. But in the coming years, as the paint begins to peel and the interest on the loans comes due, it will serve as a stark reminder of the complicated bargain many African nations have made: a gleaming present, mortgaged against an uncertain future. 

The challenge for Côte d’Ivoire, and for the dozens of other nations following the same path, will be ensuring that these monuments to sport do not become monuments to debt. Without a clear plan for post-tournament utility, the "silver crown" of Abidjan may eventually feel more like a heavy burden.

headline: With Methane and Forest Deals, Climate Summit Offers Hope After Gloomy Start

GLASGOW — After a succession of apocalyptic warnings from world leaders and a lackluster diplomatic prelude in Rome, the United Nations climate summit in Glasgow shifted on Tuesday from rhetoric to the first major policy breakthroughs of the conference.

In a pair of ambitious announcements, more than 100 nations pledged to significantly reduce emissions of methane—a potent greenhouse gas—and to halt and reverse deforestation by the end of the decade. The deals, while voluntary and facing significant hurdles in enforcement, provided a much-needed jolt of momentum to a summit that many observers feared was on the verge of stalling before it had truly begun.

The twin agreements represent the first tangible outputs of the two-week conference, known as COP26, which has been described by its hosts as the "last, best hope" to keep the goal of limiting global warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius within reach. For President Biden, who has sought to reassert American leadership on the environment after the isolationism of the Trump era, the announcements served as a platform to demonstrate that international cooperation remains possible despite deepening geopolitical rifts.

"This is a landmark commitment," Mr. Biden said during a ceremony to launch the Global Methane Pledge. He described methane as "one of the most potent greenhouse gases there is," noting that reducing its output would provide an immediate "cooling" effect on the planet.

The methane pact, led jointly by the United States and the European Union, commits signatories to cutting their collective methane emissions by 30 percent from 2020 levels by 2030. Methane is estimated to be roughly 80 times more effective at trapping heat in the atmosphere than carbon dioxide over a 20-year period, though it dissipates much faster. Scientists have long argued that curbing methane from leaky oil and gas wells, landfills, and livestock is the fastest way to slow the rate of global warming in the short term.

As of Tuesday afternoon, more than 100 countries had signed on, representing nearly half of global methane emissions and two-thirds of the global economy. However, the absence of three of the world’s top methane emitters—China, Russia, and India—cast a shadow over the proceedings, highlighting the limits of the deal's reach.

The second major breakthrough involved a massive commitment to protect the world’s forests, which act as vital "carbon sinks" by absorbing the emissions that drive climate change. More than 100 countries, containing about 85 percent of the world’s forests, pledged to end deforestation by 2030.

The agreement was bolstered by roughly $19 billion in public and private funds to support forest restoration and the protection of Indigenous lands. Notably, the signatories included Brazil, where the administration of President Jair Bolsonaro has overseen record-breaking destruction of the Amazon rainforest. The inclusion of Brazil was greeted with a mixture of surprise and skepticism by environmental groups, many of whom noted that similar past pledges, such as the 2014 New York Declaration on Forests, failed to slow the rate of tree loss.

"The world has never seen a commitment on this scale to protect our forests," said Prime Minister Boris Johnson of Britain, the host of the summit. "We have to stop the devastating loss of our forests, these great teeming ecosystems, and we have to do it by 2030."

The shift toward concrete deal-making followed a somber opening day on Monday, where leaders including Mr. Johnson and the naturalist Sir David Attenborough warned that "the clock is ticking" toward a climate catastrophe. The mood had been further dampened by a meeting of the Group of 20 in Rome over the weekend, where leaders failed to agree on a specific date for reaching net-zero emissions, settling instead on a vague commitment to do so by "mid-century."

In Glasgow, however, the diplomatic machinery appeared to be moving with more urgency. On Monday, Prime Minister Narendra Modi of India surprised the summit by announcing that his country, the world’s third-largest emitter, would reach net-zero emissions by 2070. While that date is two decades later than the 2050 target sought by the United Nations, policy analysts said the commitment was a significant step for a developing nation that has long resisted such targets.

Despite the optimism surrounding the methane and forest deals, the summit remains fraught with tension. Mr. Biden, in his remarks on Tuesday, leveled sharp criticism at his counterparts for their physical absence from the talks.

The American delegation has spent much of the summit trying to convince the world that the United States is back as a reliable partner, even as Mr. Biden’s own climate agenda faces an uncertain future in a divided Congress. "These are critical first steps, but we have seen these kinds of declarations before," said Jennifer Morgan, the executive director of Greenpeace International. The focus of the summit will now shift toward the more technical and contentious issues that have long plagued international climate negotiations. These include the "rulebook" for international carbon markets and the thorny issue of climate finance—specifically, the unfulfilled promise by wealthy nations to provide $100 billion a year to help developing countries transition to green energy and adapt to the impacts of a warming world.

The $100 billion target, originally promised by 2020, is now not expected to be met until 2023, a delay that has fueled resentment among leaders from Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific Islands. In a series of speeches on Tuesday, representatives from island nations warned that for them, the 1.5-degree target is not a diplomatic aspiration but a "death sentence" if breached.

As the heads of state began to depart Glasgow on Tuesday evening, leaving their negotiators to hash out the details of the final communiqué, the atmosphere was one of fragile progress. The gloom that had hung over the summit’s opening hours had not entirely lifted, but it had been punctured by a sense that, for the first time in years, the world’s largest economies were beginning to align on the specific, sector-by-sector interventions required to avert the worst outcomes of the climate crisis.

"We are starting to see the outlines of a path forward," said a senior European diplomat, speaking on the condition of anonymity to discuss private negotiations. "But the path is steep, and we are still at the very bottom of the mountain."

The technological sector presents another front in this economic realignment. As the Biden administration imposes sweeping export controls on semiconductors and high-tech components destined for Russia, Moscow is expected to turn to Chinese firms like Huawei and SMIC to fill the void.

Russia’s domestic tech sector is heavily dependent on Western architecture. By cutting off access to chips made with U.S. software or equipment—a rule that applies even to non-U.S. companies—the White House is attempting to degrade Russia’s industrial and military capabilities over time. China has spent years attempting to build its own self-sufficient semiconductor industry, but it still lags behind the West in the production of the most advanced chips.

The result is a forced marriage of necessity. Russia needs China’s hardware, and China sees Russia as a testing ground for a world in which it, too, might one day face similar Western isolation.

Yet, even in the realm of technology, the relationship is fraught with tension. In the past, Russian officials have expressed concern about becoming a "junior partner" or a "resource appendage" to the Chinese economy. There are also long-standing disputes over intellectual property, particularly regarding Russian military technology that China has historically reverse-engineered.

As the invasion of Ukraine enters its third day, the economic cost for Russia is mounting. The Moscow Exchange has seen record drops, and the central bank has been forced to intervene to support the ruble. The "Fortress Russia" strategy, built on the back of Chinese trade and massive reserves, was intended to give Mr. Putin the leverage to ignore Western threats.

But the speed and coordination of the Western response have been more severe than many in Moscow—and perhaps Beijing—anticipated. The decision by Germany to halt the Nord Stream 2 pipeline and the move by the U.S. to sanction Russia’s largest banks have placed an enormous burden on the China-Russia axis.

For now, Beijing is walking a fine line. It has refused to call the attack an "invasion" and has blamed the United States for "fanning the flames" of the conflict. But in the boardrooms of Beijing and Shanghai, the calculation is increasingly dominated by the risk of contagion.

The deepening of ties between the world’s largest energy exporter and its largest energy importer has created a formidable bloc, but it is one that remains tethered to a global financial system it is trying to circumvent. Whether China is willing to risk its own economic stability to sustain Russia’s war effort remains the most significant unanswered question for the global markets.

"The partnership is being tested in real-time," said Alexander Gabuev, a senior fellow at the Carnegie Moscow Center. "Beijing will provide a cushion, but it will not provide a total shield. Russia is about to find out exactly how much 'no limits' really means."

headline: Before Ukraine Invasion, Russia and China Cemented Economic Ties
generated_full_text:
As Western governments move with unexpected speed to sever Russia’s links to the global financial system, the Kremlin is looking toward a multi-billion-dollar insurance policy it spent months, if not years, negotiating with its most powerful neighbor.

But as Russian forces advance toward Kyiv and the ruble plunges to record lows, the durability of this economic axis is facing its first real-world stress test. While the strategic alignment between President Vladimir V. Putin and President Xi Jinping is firm, the commercial reality for Chinese firms and banks is fraught with risk. "The infrastructure takes years to build, but the commercial intent is clear: Moscow wants to ensure that if Europe stops buying, China is ready to take the volume."

However, the most critical element of the pre-invasion planning may be the attempt to "de-dollarize" the bilateral trade relationship. Much of the remainder is now settled in euros or the Chinese yuan.

We are likely to see a very cautious, calibrated approach from Beijing."

This caution creates an immediate problem for Russian firms. If Chinese banks refuse to issue letters of credit, the flow of physical goods—from coal to oil to timber—could stall regardless of the diplomatic agreements in place.

For Chinese technology companies, the invasion presents a different set of strategic dilemmas. As Western firms like Apple and Dell pause operations or face export controls on high-end semiconductors, Chinese giants like Huawei, Xiaomi, and Lenovo are positioned to gain market share. Yet even here, the threat of U.S. "foreign direct product" rules looms. These regulations allow Washington to block the sale of any product to Russia if it was made using U.S. software or technology, even if it was manufactured in a third country like China. If Huawei were found to be supplying Russia with equipment containing American chips or designs, it could face the same kind of devastating blacklisting that crippled its smartphone business two years ago.

The economic asymmetry of the relationship also limits Russia's leverage. While China accounts for roughly 18 percent of Russia’s total trade, Russia accounts for just over 2 percent of China’s. As the conflict in Ukraine intensifies, the cost of the "no limits" partnership is rising for both sides. For Russia, the dependency on China is no longer a strategic choice but a survival requirement. For China, the partnership offers a reliable source of energy and a fellow traveler in the effort to challenge American hegemony, but it also risks alienating its largest trading partners in Europe and the United States.

In the short term, the economic ties cemented in Beijing earlier this month will likely keep the Russian economy from total collapse. But the longer the war continues and the more aggressive the Western sanctions become, the more Chinese firms will be forced to choose between a strategic ally in Moscow and the global markets that fueled China’s own rise.

For now, the Kremlin is betting that the infrastructure of the East—the pipelines, the grain silos, and the yuan-denominated ledgers—will be enough to weather the storm from the West. But as the first few days of the war have shown, the global financial system is a more formidable opponent than any conventional military force, and China’s willingness to act as Russia’s clearinghouse has very real, very commercial limits.

headline: With Harsh Words, China’s Military Denies It Hacked Equifax

When that sovereign state denies the activity with this level of vehemence, it signals that there will be no cooperation and no end to the threat."

The 2017 hack of Equifax remains one of the most significant data breaches in history, exposing the personal information of approximately 147 million people, including Social Security numbers, birth dates, and addresses. The fallout for the company was catastrophic, leading to a massive overhaul of its leadership, a settlement with federal and state regulators reaching up to $700 million, and a total spend on security and legal costs that has exceeded $1.4 billion.

On Monday, U.S. Attorney General William P. Barr announced the indictment of the four Chinese military officers, accusing them of a "calculated and sweeping theft of the sensitive personal information of nearly half of all American citizens."

Beijing’s response on Thursday was not merely a denial but a counter-accusation. The harshness of the language reflects a broader tension that persists despite the signing of a "Phase One" trade deal in January. While that agreement was intended to pause the tariff war, it did little to address the fundamental disagreements over technology transfers, state subsidies, and cyber-espionage—issues that remain at the heart of the U.S. business community’s grievances with China.

Investors have watched these developments with growing unease. The prospect that American firms could be targeted by state actors as a matter of national policy introduces a layer of volatility that traditional risk models struggle to price. The indictment identified the defendants as members of the PLA’s 54th Research Institute. The sophistication of the attack, described in detail in court filings, has forced other financial institutions to rethink their capital expenditures on cybersecurity.

For years, many American chief executives hoped that high-level diplomatic agreements, such as the 2015 pact between President Barack Obama and President Xi Jinping to curb commercial cyber-espionage, would protect their proprietary data. However, security firms and intelligence officials have noted that while the volume of attacks may have fluctuated, the intent and capability of Chinese state actors remained consistent.

The recent denial by Beijing suggests that the 2015 consensus has effectively collapsed. By framing the Equifax indictment as a violation of international norms by the U.S., China is signaling a refusal to adhere to the American definition of "commercial" versus "national security" hacking.

"China views data as a national resource and a tool of statecraft," according to a report by a prominent risk-assessment firm distributed to clients this week. "The distinction the U.S. makes between hacking for profit and hacking for national interest is not one that Beijing recognizes in practice."

This philosophical divide has tangible consequences for the supply chains and operations of American firms. As Washington moves to restrict Chinese companies like Huawei and ZTE on security grounds, Beijing has hinted at its own "unreliable entities list," which could target American firms operating within China. Within the halls of Equifax’s Atlanta headquarters, the news of the indictment and the subsequent denial from Beijing likely provides little comfort. While its stock price has largely recovered from the post-breach lows, the company remains under a cloud of regulatory scrutiny and is operating under a consent order from the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and the Federal Trade Commission.

The company declined to comment specifically on the Chinese government’s denial on Thursday, pointing instead to earlier statements praising the Justice Department’s efforts.

The broader implications for the U.S. tech and financial sectors are more profound. The realization that even a robust defense may not be enough to thwart a state-level adversary is driving a shift in how companies approach data sovereignty. Some firms are considering "data localization," keeping sensitive information in jurisdictions perceived to be safer, though this often conflicts with the efficiency of global cloud computing.

Furthermore, the legal costs of these breaches are becoming a permanent fixture on corporate balance sheets. Insurance companies, once eager to provide cyber-liability policies, are now narrowing their coverage and raising premiums, particularly for "acts of war" or state-sponsored incidents—a classification that the Equifax indictment brings closer to reality.

headline: Halting China’s Economy Was Hard. Restarting It Is Harder.
generated_full_text:
For weeks, the world’s second-largest economy functioned as a silent, sprawling laboratory for the most aggressive quarantine in modern history. Now, as the Chinese government attempts to flip the switch back to "on," it is discovering that an industrial machine of such complexity does not restart with the same ease with which it was halted.

Across the manufacturing heartlands of Guangdong and Zhejiang provinces, the effort to resume normal operations has become a grinding exercise in frustration. While the central government in Beijing has signaled that the worst of the coronavirus outbreak has passed domestically, the reality on the factory floor is a thicket of labor shortages, broken supply chains, and a looming collapse in global demand that threatens to render the entire recovery effort moot.

Economists and supply chain analysts say the "restart" is less a surge and more a stutter. According to data from industrial sensors and satellite imagery tracking nitrogen dioxide emissions, China’s industrial capacity is operating at roughly 60 to 70 percent of its pre-lunar New Year levels. While large state-owned enterprises report higher figures, the small and medium-sized firms that form the backbone of the global supply chain remain hobbled.

"The command-and-control system is very effective at stopping things," said a senior economist at a major European bank, speaking on the condition of anonymity to discuss sensitive political dynamics. "But you cannot command a migrant worker to board a bus if he is terrified of a quarantine, and you cannot command a precision component to appear if the sub-supplier three towns over is still padlocked."

The primary obstacle remains the movement of people. China’s manufacturing model relies on roughly 290 million migrant workers, many of whom traveled to their ancestral villages for the Lunar New Year in late January and found themselves trapped by "fortress-style" lockdowns. Even as travel restrictions ease, the return to work is a bureaucratic gauntlet. Workers must often navigate a patchwork of "health code" apps, 14-day mandatory quarantines upon arrival, and local officials who are more concerned with preventing a second wave of infection than with hitting production targets.

For manufacturers, the absence of even a small fraction of the workforce can be fatal to production. In the electronics sector, where assembly lines require hundreds of discrete steps, a 20 percent vacancy rate in a factory can bring the entire line to a standstill.

"It is the 'missing bolt' problem," said supply chain consultancy firms. Even if a smartphone assembly plant in Zhengzhou is ready to go, it cannot finish a product if the specialized adhesive or the vibration motor produced in a still-shuttered facility in Hubei province is unavailable. This interconnectedness, once China’s greatest competitive advantage, has become its most significant liability.

Logistics hubs are similarly choked. While the country’s major ports remain open, the "last mile" of trucking has been decimated. Drivers, wary of being forced into quarantine if they cross provincial lines, have been slow to return to the road. Containers are piling up at docks in Shanghai and Ningbo because there are not enough drivers to haul them to inland warehouses, and not enough space to store the incoming raw materials that factories are not yet ready to process.

The financial strain on these firms is reaching a breaking point. A recent survey of small and medium-sized enterprises by researchers at Peking and Tsinghua universities found that roughly one-third of the firms surveyed had only enough cash to survive for one month. While the People’s Bank of China has pumped billions into the financial system and ordered banks to be lenient with loans, analysts say the credit is not reaching the most vulnerable companies.

"The liquidity is there at the top," said a Shanghai-based strategist. "But the risk appetite at the local bank branch level is non-existent. They don't want to lend to a textile mill that might not have any orders by May."

This brings the Chinese economy to a new, perhaps more daunting, phase of the crisis. When the lockdowns began in late January, the problem was one of supply: China could not produce goods. Now, as factories slowly whir back to life, the problem is shifting to demand.

As the coronavirus spreads rapidly through Europe and the United States, the global economy is cooling at a pace that has shocked markets. On Wednesday, the World Health Organization officially declared the outbreak a pandemic. In the United States, major events are being canceled, travel is being restricted, and consumer confidence is plummeting.

For Chinese exporters, this is a "double whammy." Just as they regain the ability to manufacture, their customers in the West are retracting. Orders for apparel, consumer electronics, and automotive parts are being canceled or postponed as retailers in New York and London brace for a recession.

"China was the first in, and it hoped to be the first out," said an analyst who tracks global trade flows. "But it is coming out into a world that is now going into the same dark room China just left. If the American consumer stops spending, it doesn't matter how many factories Beijing manages to reopen."

The geopolitical stakes of this restart are equally high. For years, the Trump administration has pressured American companies to diversify their supply chains away from China. The total shutdown of the Chinese industrial base in February provided the most compelling argument yet for "decoupling." Beijing is acutely aware that if it cannot prove it is a reliable partner—and quickly—the temporary shift of production to Vietnam, Mexico, or back to the United States could become permanent.

To counter this, the Chinese government has engaged in a high-stakes messaging campaign. State media is filled with images of workers in masks returning to clean-rooms and high-speed trains ferrying laborers back to the coast. However, there is a growing skepticism regarding the official data.

Analysts have noted a strange divergence between official "work resumption" rates—which some provinces claim are near 100 percent—and proxy measures of economic activity. Daily coal consumption at China’s six major power groups, a key indicator of industrial heat and electricity use, remains significantly below the levels seen at this time last year. Traffic congestion indices in major cities, while rising, are still far from their peaks.

In some industrial parks, local managers have reportedly kept lights and machinery running to satisfy government inspectors and electricity quotas, even if no actual production is occurring. The tension between these two goals—economic recovery and viral containment—remains the central contradiction of the restart. As the trading day closed on Thursday, with global markets in a state of freefall, the focus shifted from China’s internal logistics to the broader systemic risk. The "China shock" of February, which deprived the world of parts, is being replaced by a "global shock" in March, which deprives China of its markets.

For the factory owners of the Pearl River Delta, the hardest part was supposed to be surviving the virus. They are now realizing that surviving the recovery may be the more difficult task. The machine is starting to move again, but the ground beneath it has shifted.

headline: Halting China’s Economy Was Hard. Restarting It Is Harder.

There is also the risk of "ghost" production—where factories turn on machinery and lights to consume electricity, creating the illusion of activity to satisfy local officials who are under pressure from Beijing to report high resumption rates. Satellite imagery and data from coal consumption at power plants suggest that while activity is rebounding from the lows of February, it remains well below the levels seen during the same period last year.

If its restart falters, or if the process of reopening triggers a resurgence of the virus that necessitates a second shutdown, the prospect of a coordinated global recovery becomes increasingly remote.

In the electronics markets of Shenzhen and the textile mills of Fujian, the mood is one of wary exhaustion. Business owners describe a landscape where the rules change daily and the old certainties of the global supply chain have vanished. 

“We spent February worrying about whether our workers would come back,” said Zhang Wei, the owner of a small electronics components factory in Dongguan. “Now they are back, and we are starting to worry about whether our customers in America will still be there to take the orders. We stopped the virus, but we haven't yet saved the business.”

However, the terms of these exchanges shifted in the 1980s from permanent gifts to high-priced commercial loans, with the U.S. zoos paying up to $1 million annually per pair to the Chinese government for conservation efforts.

The Memphis agreement, signed in 2003, was a product of that era of engagement. At the time, the arrival of Ya Ya and Le Le was celebrated as a milestone for the city and a sign of China’s "opening up." But as the 20-year lease neared its end, the atmosphere changed.

In Memphis, zoo officials have expressed frustration with the narrative taking hold in China. They have maintained that Ya Ya receives world-class care and that her thin appearance is the result of a chronic skin and fur condition that is genetic, not a product of neglect. The "mistreated panda" narrative fits neatly into a broader state-driven story of Western decline and cruelty.

The logistical preparations for Ya Ya’s return are now in their final stages. She will be transported in a specialized crate, accompanied by a veterinarian and a keeper, on a flight that will take more than 15 hours. Upon arrival in Shanghai, she will undergo a month-long quarantine before being moved to the Beijing Zoo, her original home.

However, the conclusion of the Memphis loan may be a harbinger of a broader withdrawal. Currently, only three other American zoos—the National Zoo in Washington, the San Diego Zoo, and Zoo Atlanta—host giant pandas. The San Diego Zoo returned its pandas in 2019, and the National Zoo’s current agreement is set to expire at the end of 2023. If these agreements are not renewed, it is possible that for the first time in over half a century, the United States could be without any giant pandas.

Analysts suggest that Beijing may be reassessing the value of the loan program. In an era where "wolf warrior" diplomacy has replaced the "charm offensive," the need to project a cuddly image to the American public has diminished. Furthermore, the high cost of the loans and the strict requirements for bamboo sourcing and veterinary care have led some American institutions to question the long-term viability of the program.

For the residents of Memphis, the departure is a somber moment. For years, the panda exhibit was the zoo’s premier attraction, drawing millions of visitors and providing a rare point of cultural connection to a country that feels increasingly distant.

As the plane carrying Ya Ya departs, it will carry more than just a biological asset. It will take with it one of the last remaining vestiges of a period of U.S.-China relations defined by cooperation and curiosity rather than competition and confrontation. In the eyes of her Chinese fans, Ya Ya is coming home to safety. In the eyes of the world, her journey marks another step in the decoupling of two superpowers that no longer seem to speak the same language, even when the subject is as seemingly universal as the survival of a species.
